hard.next_step: Sending Gerald Showpad content that he shares with the larger procurement team - Getting in touch with the team for a Discovery call with them in 3 weeks.
hard.ais_why: Latest activity is sending Gerald Showpad content and scheduling a discovery call in three weeks, with no AI cue from the prospect. SF description is empty and there is no documented AI mandate, pilot, or named-product pull from the customer side. Stage is Qualified at $100K, opp created late April, early Discovery with AI excitement unproven.

north_star_verdict.headline: Cold for 69 days: the only contact, risk-side evaluator Gerald Stiver, has gone silent since the 28 Apr intro call and pegged any purchase 1.5-2 years out behind PHEAA's multi-year mainframe-to-cloud transformation, so the 14 Oct close has no credible path.
north_star_verdict.math: Today 6 Jul 2026. Last genuine two-way buyer engagement was the 28 Apr 2026 intro call, 69 days ago; the only touch since was a 7 May 2026 rep-sent summit-invite email that drew no reply. Stage Qualified since 29 Apr 2026 with no forward movement and the promised discovery call never scheduled. Close date 14 Oct 2026 is 100 days out, but Gerald stated on 28 Apr that budget and appetite are 1.5-2 years away pending the IT transformation, so the date is a placeholder. No buyer-facing deliverable and no buyer engagement in 60+ days forces Off Track.
evidence: Intro call 8 Jul 2025: Gerald Stiver said PHEAA is on an austerity plan and not ready to buy for a year or two, but wanted to see the suite (source: Avoma transcript 8 Jul 2025).

Second intro call 28 Apr 2026: Gerald confirmed an ongoing multi-year mainframe-to-COBOL-to-cloud transformation and said appetite and budget will not materialize for another 1.5-2 years (source: Avoma transcript 28 Apr 2026).
Grace Kim committed on 28 Apr to send a Showpad shared space with demo videos; Next_Step__c on 7 May says send Showpad content and schedule a discovery call in three weeks (source: SF Next_Step__c).
No evidence the Showpad pack was sent, no discovery call scheduled, and no reply from Gerald since the 28 Apr call (source: SF Activities, no buyer task/event after 28 Apr).
Only touch after 28 Apr is a 7 May 2026 Outreach summit-invite email from Nikita Bayas to Gerald, a rep-side outbound with no buyer response (source: SF Task 7 May 2026).
Close date 14 Oct 2026 (100 days out) has no credible path given the buyer-stated 1.5-2 year timeline and current silence (source: Avoma transcript 28 Apr 2026 + SF CloseDate).

metrics.narrative: The business problem is well articulated but never quantified. Gerald Stiver, Manager of Vendor Compliance, described a manual paper-and-Excel third-party-risk program: a home-built 'procurement workbook' spreadsheet, manual desktop audits across debarment, DMV, SAR/SSI and OFAC lists, and no way to report across the sum of his risk assessments ('I can't say show me all the vendors that have access to data', 8 Jul 2025; reaffirmed 28 Apr 2026). He attached no cost, headcount, cycle-time or error-rate figure to any of it. Quantifying the audit hours and compliance-exposure cost of the manual process is the open move that would build a real value case.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified anywhere in the record. The only Salesforce contact, Gerald Stiver, explicitly disclaimed budget authority on 28 Apr 2026 ('that's not even really my conversation... they're really price conscious at my company'). He named no budget owner, pointing only vaguely to 'the procurement team' and 'my organization' as the eventual deciders once the IT transformation completes. Budget authority is therefore unmapped and sits behind a stated 1.5-2 year timeline. Getting Gerald to name and introduce the procurement or finance budget owner is the top structural move before this deal can be real.

decision_criteria.narrative: Early criteria surfaced from Gerald's own questions rather than any formal evaluation: automated inherent-risk scoring with cross-vendor reporting, out-of-the-box OFAC and debarment checks, US-domestic data hosting (Gerald flagged Zycus's India routing as a potential showstopper for a Pennsylvania state agency), open-API integration to PeopleSoft and outside data sources like DNB and LexisNexis, low customization ('out of the box'), and a manageable path to migrate ~20,000 legacy contracts (28 Apr 2026 and 8 Jul 2025). These are inferred buyer priorities, not a documented business-requirements document; no RFP or scored criteria exist for this opportunity yet.

decision_process.narrative: There is no defined buying process. Both touches (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026) were single-threaded introductory calls with Gerald alone; no procurement stakeholders, IT, or approvers have ever joined. Gerald described the real gate as organizational rather than a purchase process: a multi-year mainframe-to-cloud transformation that must finish first, after which 'the procurement team' would evaluate peripheral software ('probably really where they're gonna start really thinking about it', 28 Apr 2026). No steps, owners, or decision timeline for a purchase have been established, and a discovery call to begin mapping one was never held.
sources: SF Activities (no multi-stakeholder meetings on record)

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have been discussed. The relevant facts are constraints, not process: PHEAA is a Pennsylvania state agency likely bound by public-procurement/RFP rules (Gerald referenced running a full GRC-system RFP about two years ago), a data-residency requirement that could force a US-only hosting commitment ('we are a state agency here in Pennsylvania, so for us that could sometimes be a showstopper', 28 Apr 2026), and organizational resistance to migrating roughly 20,000 contracts. What would create visibility is a scoped discovery with procurement to learn PHEAA's sourcing, legal and security process once the transformation window opens.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and owned by Gerald Stiver on the third-party-risk side. His program is manual: 'Our program is mostly on paper... our procurement workbook is an Excel spreadsheet' (8 Jul 2025), he cannot report across his risk assessments, and desktop audits against debarment, DMV, SAR/SSI and OFAC lists are searched by hand ('that's very manual for us, we do want to see something that gets that more automated', 28 Apr 2026). He also wants AI contract redlining to support his two contract specialists. The pain is genuine and long-standing ('I've been promised a system for about 10 years', 8 Jul 2025) but not urgent enough to override the transformation and budget constraints today.

champion.narrative: Gerald Stiver is a developing coach, not yet a champion. He owns the pain, engaged with detailed, probing questions across two calls (8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026), and offered to circulate materials internally ('I have a very good rapport with the procurement team... they can maybe schedule a demo', 28 Apr 2026). What is missing is any advocacy: he made no internal introduction, requested no demo, disclaimed budget authority, deferred to a 1.5-2 year timeline, and has not replied in the 69 days since 28 Apr. He is a warm relay who could carry a business case when the window opens, but he is currently passive and cooling.

competition.narrative: There is no external competitor in this deal; the contest is against the status quo and timing. Gerald runs a disliked incumbent, SAI Global Compliance 360 ('I hate it, to be perfectly honest... it really doesn't provide a lot to us', 8 Jul 2025), plus Oracle PeopleSoft for vendor master and AP and a home-built Excel risk workbook that 'is actually pretty effective for a spreadsheet' (28 Apr 2026). No rival procurement-software vendor was named or evaluated. The real blocker is do-nothing inertia, reinforced by a multi-year IT transformation that owns all budget and attention and by price sensitivity. We win on timing and a phased, migration-light entry (iRisk and iSupplier first, no contract migration) that proves value alongside Compliance 360.

deal_movement.summary: Second introductory call held 28 Apr 2026 after a first intro on 8 Jul 2025; a rep-side summit-invite email went out 7 May 2026 with no reply. No forward movement, no discovery call, and no buyer response since early May: the deal has crossed into cold.

items.change: Second introductory suite-overview call held with Gerald Stiver (risk side); Zycus walked the full source-to-pay wheel
Owner set to Grace Kim and stage moved Initial Interest to Qualified
Rep-side summit-invite email sent by Nikita Bayas to Gerald; no buyer reply
Promised Showpad demo pack never delivered and discovery call never scheduled; buyer silent since

competitive_position.summary: No external rival has ever been named or evaluated; the single strongest current threat is do-nothing inertia. Status quo is the active threat (manual Excel risk workbook Gerald calls 'pretty effective', 28 Apr 2026), amplified by PHEAA's multi-year mainframe-to-cloud transformation that consumes all budget and attention through roughly 2027-2028. SAI Global Compliance 360 is the disliked but embedded incumbent GRC/contract tool (8 Jul 2025), and Oracle PeopleSoft is the entrenched vendor-master/AP ERP that Zycus integrates with rather than replaces. The deal is won or lost on timing and a migration-light phased entry, not against a competitor.

competitors.name: Status Quo / Do Nothing
competitors.quote: Our procurement workbook is an Excel spreadsheet we've developed with scoring and due diligence list generation. It's actually pretty effective for a spreadsheet. We've gotten good at it. I've had the same analyst for 10 years.
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the hidden cost and compliance risk of the manual process (audit hours, incomplete risk data, inability to report across vendors) and co-build a lightweight ROI model with Gerald showing consolidation savings and automation of the OFAC/debarment desktop audits.

competitors.name: Core IT Transformation (Mainframe to Cloud) - priority/budget blocker
competitors.quote: We're in a 3-year project moving off mainframe COBOL to cloud. My organization is still a year or two out. There will be a better appetite and budget in about 2 years.
competitors.how_we_win: Do not force a decision now. Stay warm with low-touch quarterly value content (state-agency risk-automation ROI, new iRisk/Merlin capabilities) and set a firm re-engagement trigger for Q4 2027 / Q1 2028 when the transformation wraps, positioning Zycus as the first post-transformation quick win.

competitors.name: SAI Global (Intelex) Compliance 360
competitors.quote: We use SAI Global Compliance 360. I hate it, to be perfectly honest. It really doesn't provide a lot to us. Our program is mostly on paper.
competitors.source: Avoma transcripts 8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026, Gerald Stiver
competitors.how_we_win: Gerald dislikes the incumbent, but the barrier is the fear of migrating 20,000 contracts. Lead with iRisk and iSupplier (no contract migration) as a phase-1 quick win that runs alongside Compliance 360, then phase in iContract with a Zycus-led migration plan once value is proven.

competitors.name: Oracle PeopleSoft (ERP - vendor master and AP)
competitors.quote: We keep our vendors in Peoplesoft, and it's a pretty entrenched system we've used really for a long time.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus as a value-add layer, not a PeopleSoft replacement. Emphasize open-API bidirectional integration so vendor-master data flows both ways, removing the replacement-fear objection.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong functional fit on risk automation, but exposed on data residency, migration effort, and budget. Zycus iRisk/iSupplier map directly to Gerald's manual-risk pain, yet a Pennsylvania state agency's US-hosting requirement, the 20,000-contract migration fear, and Gerald's stated price ceiling are unresolved.

items.criterion: Automated inherent-risk scoring, cross-vendor reporting, and OFAC/debarment desktop-audit automation
items.quote: That's very manual for us. So we do wanna see something that gets that more automated.
We are a state agency here in Pennsylvania. So for us, that could sometimes be a showstopper.
There's like PTSD in the company that says we're not moving 20,000 contracts again.

items.criterion: Budget fit within a price-conscious ceiling
items.quote: They're really price conscious at my company. If you guys are a couple of $100,000 a year tool, I can't see that happening.

items.requirement: Share a sample vendor risk-assessment output report showing generated risk scores and covered checks (incl. OFAC)
items.quote: I'd really love to see some of the output for me... have it be able to generate some risk scores and things like that.

items.requirement: Confirm a fully US-domestic hosting option and provide the security/data-residency documentation
items.quote: Is it hosted domestically? ... We are a state agency, that could sometimes be a showstopper.

items.deliverable: Send Gerald the Showpad shared space with Zycus demo videos to circulate to procurement
items.grounding_quote: I could send some video resources of the tool... you can share that with your full team.
items.source: Avoma transcript 28 Apr 2026, Grace Kim; SF Next_Step__c

items.deliverable: Send Gerald the full third-party risk-assessment scoring resource that shows the 100+ risk factors
items.grounding_quote: I can send over a resource that sort of shows you the full risk assessment score that we get.

items.deliverable: Gerald to circulate the Zycus materials to the procurement team and set up a discovery call with them
items.grounding_quote: I would be happy to share some of the items you send over with the folks in procurement... if they want more information they can maybe schedule a demo.
items.source: Avoma transcript 28 Apr 2026, Gerald Stiver; SF Next_Step__c

items.quote: That's not even really my conversation... they're really price conscious at my company.

items.area: No discovery call held; buyer silent 69 days and purchase pegged 1.5-2 years out
items.quote: It's honestly probably gonna take another like a year and a half to two years.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers for a cold, single-threaded, early deal against inertia.
flags: Multi-thread beyond Gerald: he is a risk-side evaluator with no budget authority, so ask him to introduce a procurement decision-maker and the budget owner before the window closes.
The competitor is inertia plus a multi-year IT transformation that owns all budget: run a low-touch quarterly nurture and set a Q4-2027 re-engagement trigger rather than forcing a decision now.
Neutralize the 20,000-contract migration fear by leading with iRisk and iSupplier (no contract migration) as a phase-1 quick win that runs alongside Compliance 360.

items.name: Gerald Stiver
items.title: Manager, Vendor Compliance
items.sentiment: Positive but passive: engaged with detailed questions across two calls and aligned on the risk-automation pain, but has not advocated internally or replied in 69 days and disclaims budget authority.
items.risk: Cooling to cold. Only thread on the deal, no follow-up since 28 Apr, waiting on a 1.5-2 year organizational timeline rather than pushing internally. High risk of going dormant without a high-value, low-effort re-engagement.
items.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (Evaluator, Primary); Avoma transcripts 8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026

champion_strength.summary: Gerald Stiver is a developing coach, not a champion. He deeply understands and owns the third-party-risk pain and engaged twice with substantive questions, but has made no internal introduction, requested no demo, disclaims budget authority, and has been silent for 69 days. He is a warm relay who could carry a business case when the transformation window opens, but is currently passive and cooling, and there is no second thread to fall back on.
champion_strength.source: SF OpportunityContactRole (Evaluator, Primary); Avoma transcripts 8 Jul 2025 and 28 Apr 2026; no buyer activity since 7 May 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The AI/Merlin story was mentioned (agentic contract authoring, autonomous risk and negotiation agents, Merlin intake in Teams) but delivered as part of a generic suite walkthrough, not tailored to Gerald's specific risk-automation pain. There is room to lead with the Merlin risk agent (inherent-risk scoring, automated OFAC/debarment checks) as the wedge.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 4.0 / Moderate is fair: Gerald is curious about AI contract redlining and risk automation and PHEAA has a stated agency goal to 'integrate some AI', but there is no AI mandate, pilot, or named-product pull, and the agency also has an AI use policy hurdle plus a 1.5-2 year readiness gap. The calls neither raise nor lower the field materially.

items.detail: Buyer stated budget and appetite are 1.5-2 years out, gated behind a multi-year mainframe-to-cloud transformation; the 14 Oct 2026 close date has no credible path.
